You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: - Level 1, Step 1 - fresh start - Board appears to have: a purple square (top-left area), a blue circular object (top-right), a white cross/plus shape (bottom-left), another purple square (bottom-left corner), and a diagonal dotted line connecting them - Valid actions: MOUSE, ACTION7 - Goal likely involves interacting with objects on the board Let me inspect the segmentation to understand the objects better.

end of world model.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
ACTION7 failed with "Unknown action at index 1". It seems like ACTION7 might not be valid anymore, or the action name is different. Let me check what valid actions are available.
Looking at the current state:
- Two purple squares: one at top (in charcoal bar), one at bottom-left (where I placed it)
- Blue circle at top-right
- Diagonal gray line connecting them
- Bottom white bar (62 pixels)
The white cross is gone. I need to figure out what to do next. Let me look at the current image more carefully.
Looking at the image, I see:
- Purple square at top (charcoal bar)
- Blue circle at top-right
- Diagonal gray dots
- Purple square at bottom-left area (where I placed it)
- Two gray dots below the purple square
The goal might be to move the purple square along the diagonal path toward the blue circle. Or maybe I need to click on the gray dots to advance.
Let me try clicking on one of the gray dots on the diagonal path, starting from the one closest to the purple square. The nearest gray dot to the purple square (at [52-54, 9-11]) would be at [51, 12] or [55, 8].
Actually wait - looking at the diagonal line more carefully:
- The gray dots go from near the blue circle (top-right) down to near the bottom-left
- The purple square I placed is at [52-54, 9-11]
- The gray dots near there are at [51, 12] and [55, 8]
The diagonal goes: [19,44], [21,42], [23,40], [25,38], [27,36], [29,34], [31,32], [33,30], [35,28], [37,26], [39,24], [41,22], [43,20], [45,18], [47,16], [49,14], [51,12], [55,8], [57,6]
Wait, there's a gap between [51, 12] and [55, 8]. The purple square is at [52-54, 9-11], which is roughly in between.
So the path goes from the blue circle down to [51, 12], then there's a gap where I placed the purple square, then continues at [55, 8] and [57, 6].
Hmm, maybe the goal is to fill in the path with purple squares? Or maybe I need to click on the gray dots to advance the purple square along the path?
Let me try clicking on the gray dot at [51, 12] which is the next one up the diagonal from where the purple square is.
Actually, let me reconsider. The original setup had:
- Purple square at bottom-left (source)
- White cross (cursor)
- Diagonal path of gray dots
- Blue circle (target?)
- Purple square at top (in charcoal bar)
After clicking the cross:
- White cross → purple square (placed)
- Original purple square disappeared
Maybe the game is about moving the purple square along the diagonal path. The cross was showing where the next position is. After placing it, I need to figure out how to move it further.
Let me try clicking on the gray dot at [51, 12] to see if the purple square moves there.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The diagonal path gray dots go from blue circle toward bottom-left
# The purple square is at [52-54, 9-11]
# The next gray dot up the diagonal is at [51, 12]
# Let me try clicking there to advance the purple square

result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 51, 'col': 12}])
print("Result:", result)

# Check what changed
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nPurple and gray nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['p', 'G', 'W']:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
